examples.title: Configure VM CPU and Memory with libvirt
examples.description: Python script to create a VM with specific CPU and memory configuration
examples.code:
import libvirt

conn = libvirt.open('qemu:///system')

# VM Configuration with detailed CPU and memory settings
vm_xml = '''
<domain type="kvm">
  <name>webapp-server</name>
  
  <!-- Memory Configuration -->
  <memory unit="GiB">8</memory>
  <currentMemory unit="GiB">4</currentMemory>
  
  <!-- CPU Configuration -->
  <vcpu placement="static" current="2">4</vcpu>
  <cpu mode="host-passthrough">
    <topology sockets="1" cores="2" threads="2"/>
  </cpu>
  
  <!-- Memory Balloon for Dynamic Memory -->
  <memballoon model="virtio">
    <stats period="10"/>
  </memballoon>
  
  <os>
    <type arch="x86_64" machine="q35">hvm</type>
    <boot dev="hd"/>
  </os>
  
  <devices>
    <!-- Virtio disk for better performance -->
    <disk type="file" device="disk">
      <driver name="qemu" type="qcow2" cache="writeback"/>
      <source file="/var/lib/libvirt/images/webapp.qcow2"/>
      <target dev="vda" bus="virtio"/>
    </disk>
    
    <!-- Virtio network for better performance -->
    <interface type="network">
      <source network="default"/>
      <model type="virtio"/>
    </interface>
  </devices>
</domain>
'''

# Create the domain
dom = conn.defineXML(vm_xml)
print(f"VM '{dom.name()}' defined")
print(f"  Max Memory: {dom.maxMemory()} KB")
print(f"  vCPUs: {dom.vcpusFlags(libvirt.VIR_DOMAIN_VCPU_MAXIMUM)}")

# Start the VM
dom.create()
print(f"VM '{dom.name()}' started")

conn.close()
examples.explanation: This script creates a KVM VM with detailed configuration: 8GB max memory with 4GB current allocation (for dynamic memory), 4 vCPUs with 2 active, host CPU passthrough, and virtio devices for optimal performance.

examples.title: Create Virtual Disks (QCOW2 and RAW)
examples.description: Shell commands to create different types of virtual disks
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Create Virtual Disks for VMs

DISK_DIR="/var/lib/libvirt/images"

echo "=== Creating Virtual Disks ==="

# 1. Create thin-provisioned QCOW2 disk (grows as needed)
echo "Creating thin-provisioned QCOW2 (50GB)..."
qemu-img create -f qcow2 "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2" 50G
ls -lh "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2"  # Shows actual size ~200KB

# 2. Create QCOW2 with preallocation (better performance)
echo "
Creating preallocated QCOW2 (50GB)..."
qemu-img create -f qcow2 -o preallocation=metadata "$DISK_DIR/prealloc-disk.qcow2" 50G

# 3. Create RAW disk (thick provisioned, best performance)
echo "
Creating RAW disk (20GB)..."
qemu-img create -f raw "$DISK_DIR/raw-disk.img" 20G
# To fully allocate: fallocate -l 20G "$DISK_DIR/raw-disk.img"

# 4. Convert between formats
echo "
Converting QCOW2 to RAW..."
qemu-img convert -f qcow2 -O raw "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2" "$DISK_DIR/converted.raw"

# 5. Get disk information
echo "
=== Disk Information ==="
qemu-img info "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2"

# 6. Create a snapshot
echo "
=== Creating Snapshot ==="
qemu-img snapshot -c "baseline" "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2"
qemu-img snapshot -l "$DISK_DIR/thin-disk.qcow2"
examples.explanation: Demonstrates creating different virtual disk types: thin-provisioned QCOW2 (space-efficient), preallocated QCOW2 (better performance), RAW format (best performance), and disk conversion/snapshot operations.

examples.title: Live Migration with libvirt
examples.description: Python script to perform live VM migration between hosts
examples.code:
import libvirt
import sys

def live_migrate_vm(vm_name, dest_host):
    """Perform live migration of a VM to another host"""
    
    # Connect to source hypervisor
    src_conn = libvirt.open('qemu:///system')
    if src_conn is None:
        print("Failed to connect to source hypervisor")
        return False
    
    # Connect to destination hypervisor
    dest_uri = f'qemu+ssh://root@{dest_host}/system'
    dest_conn = libvirt.open(dest_uri)
    if dest_conn is None:
        print(f"Failed to connect to destination: {dest_host}")
        return False
    
    # Get the domain to migrate
    try:
        dom = src_conn.lookupByName(vm_name)
    except libvirt.libvirtError:
        print(f"VM '{vm_name}' not found")
        return False
    
    print(f"Starting live migration of '{vm_name}' to {dest_host}")
    print(f"  VM State: {'Running' if dom.isActive() else 'Stopped'}")
    print(f"  Memory: {dom.maxMemory() // 1024} MB")
    
    # Migration flags
    flags = (
        libvirt.VIR_MIGRATE_LIVE |           # Live migration
        libvirt.VIR_MIGRATE_PERSIST_DEST |   # Define VM on destination
        libvirt.VIR_MIGRATE_UNDEFINE_SOURCE  # Remove from source after
    )
    
    # Optional: Set bandwidth limit (MB/s)
    bandwidth = 100  # 100 MB/s
    
    try:
        # Perform migration
        new_dom = dom.migrate(
            dest_conn,
            flags,
            None,  # New name (None = keep same)
            None,  # URI (None = auto)
            bandwidth
        )
        
        if new_dom:
            print(f"Migration successful!")
            print(f"VM now running on: {dest_host}")
            return True
        else:
            print("Migration failed")
            return False
            
    except libvirt.libvirtError as e:
        print(f"Migration error: {e}")
        return False
    finally:
        src_conn.close()
        dest_conn.close()

# Usage
live_migrate_vm('webapp-server', 'hypervisor2.example.com')
examples.explanation: This script performs live migration of a running VM between KVM hosts. It uses libvirt flags for live migration, persisting on destination, and removing from source. Requires shared storage and SSH connectivity between hosts.

examples.title: VM Snapshot Management (PowerShell)
examples.description: PowerShell commands for Hyper-V snapshot/checkpoint management
examples.code:
# Hyper-V Snapshot (Checkpoint) Management

$VMName = "WebServer"

# Create a snapshot before making changes
Write-Host "Creating snapshot before update..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
$checkpoint = Checkpoint-VM -Name $VMName -SnapshotName "Pre-Update-$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmm')" -Passthru
Write-Host "Checkpoint created: $($checkpoint.Name)"

# List all snapshots for a VM
Write-Host "`nCurrent snapshots:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Get-VMSnapshot -VMName $VMName | Format-Table Name, CreationTime, ParentSnapshotName

# Get snapshot tree
Write-Host "`nSnapshot hierarchy:" -ForegroundColor Cyan
function Show-SnapshotTree {
    param([string]$VMName, [string]$ParentId = $null, [int]$Indent = 0)
    
    $snapshots = Get-VMSnapshot -VMName $VMName | Where-Object {
        if ($ParentId) { $_.ParentSnapshotId -eq $ParentId }
        else { $_.ParentSnapshotId -eq $null }
    }
    
    foreach ($snap in $snapshots) {
        Write-Host (" " * $Indent + "├── $($snap.Name)") -ForegroundColor Yellow
        Show-SnapshotTree -VMName $VMName -ParentId $snap.Id -Indent ($Indent + 4)
    }
}
Show-SnapshotTree -VMName $VMName

# Restore to a snapshot (revert changes)
Write-Host "`nReverting to snapshot..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
$latestSnapshot = Get-VMSnapshot -VMName $VMName | Sort-Object CreationTime -Descending | Select-Object -First 1
Restore-VMSnapshot -VMSnapshot $latestSnapshot -Confirm:$false
Write-Host "Reverted to: $($latestSnapshot.Name)"

# Delete old snapshots (keep last 3)
Write-Host "`nCleaning up old snapshots..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
$snapshotsToDelete = Get-VMSnapshot -VMName $VMName | 
    Sort-Object CreationTime -Descending | 
    Select-Object -Skip 3

foreach ($snap in $snapshotsToDelete) {
    Write-Host "Removing: $($snap.Name)"
    Remove-VMSnapshot -VMSnapshot $snap
}

Write-Host "`nSnapshot management complete!" -ForegroundColor Green
examples.explanation: PowerShell script for managing Hyper-V checkpoints (snapshots). Creates snapshots before updates, displays snapshot hierarchy, reverts to previous states, and cleans up old snapshots to save disk space.
